author: @brianfoley-v3w
text: Drive your car up on a platform that is connected to a couple winch generators and they can lower the car to ground level while using the car's weight to drive the generator. Lights and heat off grid!

text: Just found found out this concept has been exploited by a company called Gravitricity to store electricity in deep mine shafts.

author: @arodhall11
text: You could use a handcrank to lift with pulleys but that requires attention 😄

author: @arodhall11
text: Use one winch with 4+ pulleys to lift 2000 lbs up 30 feet, then a second winch drops with gearing slowly and is used as a generator. Two setups can alternate assuring continuous electric supply.

replies.author: @jamesharrell9096
replies.text:
If it were that easy, everyone would be doing it.

In reality, your system could store potential energy in the form of gravity, but it would be less than 100% efficient. You would always get back less energy than you put in as some would unavoidably be lost as heat. And energy can't be created or destroyed, only transformed, so it has to come from somewhere.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
chuckle factor...  rather, a wind driven winch to lift an offline weight - alternate weights allow "continuous output" at the "repurlosed motor...- or use the planetary gear as an on demand differential output (needs 2 brakes/dogs for maximum effectiveness) - either driving to storage, or to power output (can combine wind and weigh to generate powert as desired) - ok this gets stupid, fun for a hobby set to learn how things work, solar is usually a better fit for real useful systems.  lol..

(when you realise the magnitude of chemical energy we use - hundreds of tonnes mass dozens of metres high - for gravity equivalent, and that is just a meagre daily allowance, boggles the mind.)

author: @edkoetsier339
text: Easier and better just to get a large DC or 3 phase permanent magnet or brushless motor and put a modest pully or chain drive on it. You will get 24v and 10 amps minimum in a stiff breeze. I did that successfully with Amatek motors (and got up to a 1000w) before I moved onto Axial Flux. But cool nonetheless.

author: @davestech6357
text: A treadmill can be had for cheap or free. The 90 volt or greater motor is well suited for a generator and easily used without all the trouble and they usually have a flywheel too.  Also in your neck of the woods I think they use  beefy brushless motors in washing machines that are even better to be used.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: The next time you tear down a junk car, take the flywheel and starter motor.  There's already a gear set attached, and the starter solenoid can be fixed at the engaged position, giving you +/- 40:1 gear ratio for a high-capacity generator.  You could also tear down the transmission for other gearing, shafting and bearings.  Good video, Thanks!

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: If it's only 11:1 you should be able to get it going with a modest lengthed lever I'd have thought. Did you try turning it with anything other that the 2" drum? I'd have thought a 1ft crank(/pedal?) would get that going
